summary:
The Mughal-Maratha Wars (1681–1707 AD) were a protracted conflict in which the [[event:103|Mughal Emperor]] Aurangzeb spent the last 27 years of his life and essentially the entire military and financial resources of his empire campaigning in the Deccan against the [[event:2368|Maratha Confederacy]] — a campaign that achieved tactical victories while producing strategic catastrophe, exhausting the Mughal state and enabling both Maratha expansion and ultimately British conquest of India. Aurangzeb personally moved his court and army to the Deccan in 1681, determined to crush the Maratha state and extend Mughal sovereignty to the southern tip of the subcontinent.

Aurangzeb captured Sambhaji (son of Shivaji) in 1689 and had him publicly tortured and executed — an act that united Maratha resistance rather than breaking it. Despite capturing the major Maratha fortresses and nominally controlling vast territories, Aurangzeb found that the Maratha cavalry simply evaded set-piece battles, raided Mughal supply lines, and reasserted control whenever Mughal armies moved on. Maratha guerrilla warfare made permanent conquest impossible. By the time of Aurangzeb's death at Ahmadnagar in 1707 at the age of 88, the Mughal treasury was bankrupt, the army demoralized, and Mughal authority rapidly collapsing across the subcontinent. The Deccan wars had destroyed the Mughal fiscal-military system: the revenue drain from maintaining a vast army in the south while revenues collapsed in the north created a fiscal deficit from which the empire never recovered. Within 50 years of Aurangzeb's death, the Marathas were effectively administering the nominal Mughal emperor in Delhi — a complete reversal of the campaign's intent.
